# Cool, you made it!

> Let's talk process improvement.
---

## Intro

Sometimes known as DragBallots. Sometimes known as AgentChiefLou.

- **Role**: Business Analytics Consultant at PNC
- **Learning**: Agentic Workflows, RAG concepts (within the scope of token cost optimization).
- **Working On**: A football game that I'll eventually publish on STEAM, custom Audio VSTs and plugins, and a DAW. 
- **Current Place I'm Respirating At**: Raleigh, NC. 

---

## Latest Work Accomplishments
  - Migration of an asset class off a legacy mainframe and onto an internal one.
  - 2 Web Scrapers sourcing, grouping, and parsing documents as part of a remediaiton effort.
  - Enterprise Wide Dashboard Reporting in Power BI
  - Grid-wide usage analytics allowing DBAs to monitor table usage.
---

## Fun Projects

### Play Sports
A game I want to eventually publish on Steam. That's all for now. 

- **Tech:** Unreal Engine, C++, C -- completely built through workflows. 
- **Repository:** [Project Name](https://github.com/yourusername/project)
- **Stats:** https://play-sports.vercel.app/api?username=agentchieflou

---

### NoProd
DAW, like SUNO, but you still have to have some idea on how to make songs. So it's SUNO a creator might actually like. 

- **Tech:** Typescript, C, python, etc., 
- **Repository:** [Project Name](https://github.com/yourusername/project)
- **Stats:** https://noprod.vercel.app/api?username=agentchieflou

---
## Connect
- LinkedIn: [https://linkedin.com/in/yourname](https://www.linkedin.com/in/michael-louard)
- GitHub: https://github.com/agentchieflou
- Email: mbf.louard@gmail.com
---

> *"When in doubt, question the doubt, deny the doubt, then stop doubting."*

[{"sha":"afbe6d970a1d90f20f4f00c364067050e4d76410","node_id":"C_kwDOTdUAj9oAKGFmYmU2ZDk3MGExZDkwZjIwZjRmMDBjMzY0MDY3MDUwZTRkNzY0MTA","commit":{"author":{"name":"agentchieflou","email":"mbf.louard@gmail.com","date":"2026-07-20T14:31:19Z"},"committer":{"name":"agentchieflou","email":"mbf.louard@gmail.com","date":"2026-07-20T14:31:19Z"},"message":"Rolodex job cards, skill-match gate, rejected-job feedback loop, remote-detection fix

- skills.html: replace the side detail panel with a fanned rolodex card stack
  (front + 2 neighbors in DOM, click/swipe/arrow/Escape nav).
- agent: MIN_SKILL_MATCHES gate drops jobs with <3 matched skills from the
  digest/graph; agent/rejected.py mirrors applied.py (CA-REJECTED mailto +
  IMAP sync) and feeds a Rocchio negative centroid in rank.py so rejected
  postings suppress structurally similar future ones.
- fetch_jobs/util: looks_genuinely_remote() replaces the naive "remote"
  substring check and rejects hybrid/onsite/relocation phrasing; fetch_all()
  drops any non-remote job as defense in depth.
- current_resume.docx resynced from resume.html (was missing the current role).

skills page: demand matrix + match ledger; home/nav cleanup

- skills.html: Demand Matrix as the landing view, Match Ledger toggle;
  force-graph canvas removed. Sort jobs by match / position A-Z /
  company A-Z, skill columns by demand / A-Z; legend chips filter by
  category (incl. skill gap). Kate mode, geodesic backdrop, detail
  panel, and blocklist filter retained.
- index.html: email next to the ML monogram (Find Me email entry
  removed), "Skills Graph" renamed to "Skills", home meta line now has
  a one-click resume link + latest GitHub commit datetime (reuses the
  existing repos fetch).
- resume.html: nav label renamed to "Skills".

agent: applied tracking, strict salary rule, doomers source, ATS enrichment

- Skill blocklist (SAS/Stata/NLP-BERT) enforced in profile extraction and
  mirrored client-side in skills.html
- Digest now top 10; postings must state a salary (field or posting text)
  reaching $130k — everything else is dropped
- New source: doomersareretardedcommunists.com daily snapshots (manifest
  check per run, group files fetched once per snapshot day, cached parse)
- Email-driven applied tracking: "mark applied" mailto links -> CA-APPLIED
  self-emails -> IMAP sync -> applied.json (opaque ids only, public branch);
  applied jobs excluded from digest and graph
- Finalist enrichment via ATS public JSON APIs (Greenhouse/Lever/
  SmartRecruiters/Workday/Ashby); ATS-confirmed-gone postings dropped
- Cumulative "qualifying jobs found to date" counter exported to the graph
  and shown on skills.html

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 # play-sports

AI-driven, physics-based football game project scaffold for Unreal Engine 5.

## Project Structure

- `play-sports.uproject` - Unreal Engine project definition.
- `Source/PlaySports` - main game module.
- `Plugins/Autonomix` - AI editor bypass plugin stub for T3D injection and Python scripting.
- `Plugins/AgenticLink` - external agent bridge plugin stub.
- `Data` - external data assets and ingestion sources.

## Implemented Systems

- `PSPlayerAttributes` - player attribute `USTRUCT` for data tables.
- `PSPlaySimulation` - headless play simulation state machine.
- `PSDataIngestion` - JSON-to-DataTable ingestion utility.
- `PSScheduleEngine` - programmatic 18-week season schedule generator.
- `PSFunctionalGym` - starting point for automated functional tests.

## Agentic Workflow

This repo is set up to be worked on by multiple AI coding tools (Claude Code, Antigravity,
GitHub Copilot, and free-tier models like Ollama/Gemini/OpenRouter) with shared context. See
[`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) for the architecture map, conventions, and how each tool's connectors
are wired up.

## Roadmap

Development is tracked in [`ROADMAP.md`](ROADMAP.md): a 25-Epic core across 5 phases, sequenced
vertical-slice first (one crude but complete play end-to-end, then deepen each system), plus 100
expansion Epics (26–125) in themed track files under [`roadmap/`](roadmap/) — broadcast overlays,
cameras, stadium atmosphere, animation depth, gameplay nuance, AI, franchise, audio, UX,
multiplayer, engineering infrastructure, and content generation. Each expansion Epic is sized
(S/M/L/XL) and moded (code/editor/mixed) for agent scoping.

character archetypes, hitpoints, and combat rules (Epics 139-141)

Introduces the proprietary combat layer requested for future multiplayer
support: every on-field player has hitpoints (UPSHealthComponent +
UPSCombatRulesModel's deterministic damage rolls), a tackle no longer
unconditionally ends the play -- it deals damage, and the play only ends
once the ball carrier is downed. Downed carriers sit out exactly the next
play; non-carriers respawn at full health immediately (UPSRoster's new
FPSPlayerLiveState, extending its existing player-availability authority).
Interceptions auto-kill the QB's intended receiver rather than whoever the
ball hits. Punting is disallowed and the defense fields an extra player on
4th down (UPSRulesConfig). Player leveling/XP (UPSPlayerLeveling) grows
per-archetype attributes as a future microtransaction seam, gameplay only.

Also documents Track Q (roadmap/character-combat.md, Epics 139-141), a
recommended MVP priority sequencing across the code-mode expansion tracks
in ROADMAP.md, and answers (in the accompanying plan) why Epic 22's
animation work can't proceed without a human- or bridge-driven Editor
session.

generalized data ingestion, validation, sample league (Epic 21)

Fixes a compile break in the previous commit (UFUNCTION inside a
USTRUCT is invalid -- FPSTeamStanding::GetWinPercentage() is now a
plain inline method).

UPSDataIngestion generalized beyond players: LoadTeamsFromJson,
LoadLeagueConfigFromJson (FPSLeagueConfig), plus
ValidatePlayersJson/ValidateTeamsJson returning "Row N: <field>
<problem>" actionable errors (missing PlayerId, unrecognized Role
enum string, negative numeric attributes, duplicate/missing TeamId).
UPSContentReimportCommandlet re-imports and validates every Data/
file in one action. Sample league dataset: 4 teams (Falcons + new
Hawks/Wolves/Bears rosters, ~11 players each covering every
EPlayerRole) plus sample_league_config.json. Data/README.md documents
the full contract for external tools/agents authoring content.

AI controller state authoritative in-memory; add Season/Franchise (Epic 20)

The previous fix (constructor-time Blackboard component) made the
component non-null but BB->InitializeBlackboard() on the dynamically-
synthesized UBlackboardData still returns false on this engine build
(root cause not identified without editor/debugger access -- flagged
for follow-up once real .uasset BlackboardData can be authored in
editor). Rather than keep guessing at engine internals blind,
APSOffenseController/APSDefenseController now treat plain in-memory
members as the authoritative state (CurrentPlayPhaseValue,
bHasPossessionValue, RouteWaypoints/CurrentWaypointIndex,
CurrentAssignment) with new BlueprintPure getters; the Blackboard
writes remain as a best-effort mirror for future editor-authored BT
assets but nothing depends on them succeeding. Updated all AI tests
to assert against the getters instead of raw Blackboard reads.

Epic 20: UPSFranchiseSeason (round-robin matchup generation over
UPSScheduleEngine's weeks, standings tracking), UPSQuickSimRunner
(drives UPSPlaySimulation's existing quick-sim rolls headlessly to a
final score, reusing it as the single authority on play outcomes
rather than duplicating scoring logic), UPSPlayoffBracket (seeded
single-elimination bracket from final standings), UPSFranchiseSaveGame
(persists season state through the existing UPSSaveSubsystem).

pre-existing Blackboard init failure; add roster/progression/injury (Epic 19)

Root-cause fix: APSOffenseController/APSDefenseController lazily created
their Blackboard component via UseBlackboard() inside OnPossess. In
headless/automation-test worlds that runtime-created component is never
registered, so UseBlackboard() silently fails and every key resolves to
KeyID 65535 (invalid) -- this was failing
PlaySports.AI.OffenseControllerTelemetrySync on every CI run back to
2deebdc, before this session's changes, and newly broke my Epic 15/17
tests that inherited the same pattern. Fix: create the Blackboard
component as a constructor-time CreateDefaultSubobject (the standard
AIController idiom) so it's always registered when the actor spawns,
and call BB->InitializeBlackboard() directly.

Epic 19: UPSRoster (53-player roster + ordered depth chart per role,
fatigue-driven backup substitution reading the Epic 6 stamina hooks),
UPSPlayerProgression (age-curve growth/decline, data-driven via
FPSProgressionTuning), UPSInjuryModel (fatigue-scaled per-play injury
probability/severity, seedable for replay). FPSPersonnelPackage data
shape defined but not yet wired to formation resolution -- left
unchecked on the roadmap.

coaching & play-selection AI (Epic 18)

UPSCoachingAI weights play categories from an FPSSituationContext
(down/distance/clock/score) and a data-driven FPSTendencyProfile
(aggression + per-category weight table), then weighted-randomly
selects among matching plays with a seedable RandomStream for
deterministic replay. 4th-down, 2-point, and clock-management
decision helpers. IPSCoachingSuggestionProvider is a designed-but-
unimplemented LLM hook -- gated behind the AgenticLink bridge
(Plugins/AgenticLink is still a stub), falls back to internal
weighting when no provider is registered. PlayCategory field added
to FPSPlayDefinition and populated across the starter playbook.

22-agent play orchestration and route-waypoint API (Epic 17)

UPSPlayOrchestrator distributes a selected FPSPlayDefinition into
per-pawn route/coverage assignments across all on-field pawns, role
by role, deduplicating repeated role slots. Adds
APSOffenseController::SetAssignedRoute/AdvanceToNextWaypoint (Target
Location + RouteWaypointIndex blackboard keys per the offense AI
spec) so the orchestrator has a real API to drive. Scramble-drill
broken-play adaptation and a seedable FRandomStream for deterministic
replay. Starter playbook expanded to 8 offensive / 6 defensive plays.

playbook data schema and JSON ingestion (Epic 16)

FPSPlayDefinition/FPSPlayAssignment/FPSRoute DataTable rows plus
UPSPlaybookIngestion mirroring UPSDataIngestion's JSON-to-DataTable
pattern. Starter playbook: 6 offensive plays, 4 defensive calls, and
a 6-route waypoint library covering pass/run/coverage/blitz branches.

# v-loudio-t
Virtual Studio Technologies, created by Lou, so they're Virtual Loudio Technoligies.

[{"sha":"540ae0739bb19536730ab9dbc033571fea8552f1","node_id":"C_kwDOTa7z5NoAKDU0MGFlMDczOWJiMTk1MzY3MzBhYjlkYmMwMzM1NzFmZWE4NTUyZjE","commit":{"author":{"name":"Michael Louard","email":"mbf.louard@gmail.com","date":"2026-07-18T11:56:20Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-07-18T11:56:20Z"},"message":"B3: Extract ReverbCore from JUCE/APVTS coupling (#109)

Extracts the Reverb's actual DSP engine into a new JUCE-free ReverbCore
class, so it can eventually be hosted by something other than JUCE/
APVTS -- specifically the future LPI-ABI wrapper (Epic B, #97).
LoudioReverbProcessor thins down to exactly two bridges: APVTS<->
ReverbCore parameter sync each block, and ReverbCore's analyzer output
-> dspToUiRingBuffer. Every formula in ReverbCore::process() is a
line-for-line port of the pre-extraction processBlock(), cross-checked
directly against the prior commit's source.

Three JUCE-free replacements, following the existing stb_truetype.h
single-header-vendoring precedent (Plugins/Reverb/
ARCHITECTURE_DECISIONS.md #2) where a from-scratch implementation was
the more self-contained/auditable choice over vendoring an unverified
third-party dependency:
- SimpleFFT: standard radix-2 Cooley-Tukey complex FFT (juce::dsp::FFT
  replacement). Not real-only optimized, but at 1024 points once per
  UI-refresh-rate-ish cadence this is not remotely a bottleneck.
- SimpleBiquad: RBJ Audio EQ Cookbook shelf/peak formulas
  (juce::dsp::IIR::Coefficients replacement), the well-established
  public reference JUCE's own coefficient generation closely follows.
- SimpleSmoothedValue: linear ramp smoothing (juce::LinearSmoothedValue
  replacement), identical semantics.

New shared parameter table (SharedStructures.h's kReverbParams[]) is
the single source of truth for every parameter's id/name/range/
default/step -- both createParameterLayout() and ReverbCore now read
from it, so the numbers can't independently drift. Cross-checked every
single value against the prior commit's hardcoded createParameterLayout()
by direct diff, confirming byte-for-byte identical parameter ids,
ranges, steps, and defaults -- APVTS's XML serialization format (and
therefore existing preset/session compatibility) is unchanged by
construction, not just by assumption.

ReverbCore exposes two ways to set a parameter, matching two genuinely
different threading situations: setParameterValueDirect() (same-thread,
immediate -- what the JUCE wrapper uses every block, since it already
has APVTS as a thread-safe value source) and setParameterValue()
(RT-safe, ring-buffered, matching the LPI ABI's set_parameter_value
contract exactly -- unused by the JUCE path today, ready for the
future LPI wrapper). getState()/setState() is a separate small binary
blob for a host with no APVTS-equivalent preset mechanism at all;
LoudioReverbProcessor's own getStateInformation/setStateInformation
keep using APVTS's existing XML shape completely unchanged.

Scope note: this issue's plan also described extracting PluginEditor.
cpp's widget-construction code behind a shared ParamBridge abstraction,
for the JUCE wrapper and a future LPI offscreen GUI to both reuse.
Deferred to B4 (#101) instead of done speculatively here -- B4 is where
a real second consumer (the LPI GUI) will actually exist to design
that abstraction against, rather than guessing its shape now with
nothing to verify it against.

9 new tests in DSPTests.cpp (18/18 total) exercising ReverbCore
directly: param table index integrity, defaults match the shared
table, both parameter-set paths (immediate and ring-buffered), a
zero-tolerance dryWet=0.0 bit-exact passthrough check, white-noise
stability (finite/bounded over 8192 samples), freeze-mode tail-energy
sustain, state save/load round-trip, and analyzer dirty-flag lifecycle.

Verified: full dsp_tests (18/18) and a real Standalone rebuild --
PID-verified screenshots confirm default parameter values match the
shared table exactly, a real WM_LBUTTONDOWN/MOUSEMOVE/UP drag on the
Decay Time knob correctly changes its value (0.11 -> 0.41) end-to-end
through the ring-buffer -> APVTS -> ReverbCore path, and all 10
Advanced-tab knobs (post-EQ, crossover, ducking, gating -- all now
SimpleBiquad/ReverbCore-backed) render correctly.

Found and fixed two build issues along the way: a name collision with
juce::SmoothedValue (renamed to SimpleSmoothedValue, matching the
Simple* naming already established by SimpleBiquad/SimpleFFT), and
windows.h's min/max macros (pulled in transitively via cuif/
ring_buffer.h) clashing with std::max/std::min in the gate-fade clamp
(replaced with plain conditionals).

Part of #97.

Louard","email":"mbf.louard@gmail.com","date":"2026-07-18T11:33:26Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-07-18T11:33:26Z"},"message":"B2: cuif offscreen rendering mode (#108)

Extends cuif_window with an offscreen render path -- a new
cuif_window_create_offscreen() creates a hidden HWND (same "backing
window nobody ever sees" trick already used to bootstrap the
process-wide root GL context) whose GL context targets an FBO-backed
RGBA8 color texture instead of that HWND's own on-screen framebuffer.
cuif_window_render_frame() renders into it exactly as normal (widget/
graphics code never touches window->hwnd/hdc directly, so it's
completely unaware of the distinction); cuif_window_read_pixels()
reads the result back instead of a real window ever presenting
anything via SwapBuffers.

New cuif_window_inject_mouse_down/up/move() call the exact same
cuif_widget_dispatch_mouse_*() functions cuif_wnd_proc() calls for
real WM_LBUTTONDOWN/UP/WM_MOUSEMOVE messages, just without an actual
Win32 message ever being sent -- this is the synthetic input path a
future frame-streaming input relay (browser-forwarded mouse events)
will call into.

MSAA is not requested for offscreen windows in this version (FBO
multisample-resolve is unimplemented) -- cuif's adaptive tessellation
already reduces aliasing enough that this is a reasonable v1 scope
cut. FBO functions (glGenFramebuffers etc., GL 3.0 core /
ARB_framebuffer_object) are resolved via wglGetProcAddress, same
pattern already used for MSAA and glGenerateMipmap resolution
elsewhere in this file.

Intended for hosts with no native window surface to embed into (e.g.
noprod's browser-based UI) -- see Plugins/Reverb/
ARCHITECTURE_DECISIONS.md and Epic B (LPI).

New standalone test harness (offscreen_harness.c, since there's no
visible window to screenshot the normal way): builds a knob widget,
renders a frame, injects a real synthetic drag (down + 20 move steps
+ up), renders again, dumps both frames to disk as BMP. Visually
confirmed: knob value climbs from 0.30 to 0.60 exactly matching the
drag, text renders crisp at 1.5x DPI. Caught and fixed two real
ordering bugs along the way while building this harness: (1) loading
the font before the window/GL context existed (the same class of
context-timing bug fixed elsewhere in this codebase -- must load
after the window makes its context current), (2) needing the same
DPI rebake step PluginEditor.cpp already does, without which text
renders at the wrong effective resolution for a non-1.0x scale.

3 new automated tests in cuif_tests.c (28/28 total): FBO render +
read_pixels() returns the correct theme background color,
read_pixels() rejects an undersized buffer and a non-offscreen
window, and injected mouse input reaches a widget's callback
identically to a real WM_LBUTTONDOWN.

Verified: full cuif_tests (28/28) and dsp_tests (9/9) suites pass;
Standalone rebuilt and screenshot-verified with no visual regression
(this touches window_win32.c, shared with the VST3/Standalone build).

Part of #97.

context-timing bug fixed elsewhere in this codebase -- must load
after the window makes its context current), (2) needing the same
DPI rebake step PluginEditor.cpp already does, without which text
renders at the wrong effective resolution for a non-1.0x scale.

3 new automated tests in cuif_tests.c (28/28 total): FBO render +
read_pixels() returns the correct theme background color,
read_pixels() rejects an undersized buffer and a non-offscreen
window, and injected mouse input reaches a widget's callback
identically to a real WM_LBUTTONDOWN.

Verified: full cuif_tests (28/28) and dsp_tests (9/9) suites pass;
Standalone rebuilt and screenshot-verified with no visual regression
(this touches window_win32.c, shared with the VST3/Standalone build).

Louard","email":"mbf.louard@gmail.com","date":"2026-07-18T10:59:16Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-07-18T10:59:16Z"},"message":"B1: LPI Plugin ABI v1 (#106)

New PluginABI/ -- a dependency-free C header (lpi.h, extern "C",
C-linkage vtable, opaque lpi_plugin* handle), physically separate from
cuif, intended as the primary plugin format for noprod (VST3/JUCE
remains the Ableton/third-party-DAW compatibility bridge).

Core lpi_plugin_api: create/destroy/activate/deactivate/process (fixed
sample rate set once at create, matching every DSP object in this
codebase already following a one-time prepare(sampleRate, blockSize)
contract) plus parameter enumeration/get/set and versioned binary
state save/load. Growth without ABI breaks goes through a
string-ID-keyed get_extension() capability query -- a host that
doesn't know an extension id gets NULL back, a plugin that doesn't
implement one returns NULL too.

set_parameter_value() is RT-safe by ABI contract: real plugins must
internally enqueue via a lock-free SPSC ring buffer (the same pattern
Framework/include/cuif/ring_buffer.h and VSTs/Reverb/src/
PluginProcessor.cpp already use for audio<->UI communication), so
hosts never need their own locking around it.

Deliverables:
- lpi.h -- the ABI header, header-only INTERFACE CMake target
- examples/toy_gain -- a trivial single-parameter gain plugin DLL
  validating the ABI shape before Reverb's complexity is involved
- test/host_harness -- loads the toy plugin purely via LoadLibrary +
  GetProcAddress("lpi_get_factory"), no compile-time link dependency,
  proving dynamic loading end to end
- test/rt_safety -- a real two-thread stress test (one producer thread
  hammering set_parameter_value with 200k sequential values while the
  main thread calls process() in a tight loop), asserting the applied
  value sequence is always monotonic and never torn

Found and fixed a real bug during RT-safety testing: cuif_spsc_write()
does partial writes when the ring buffer is nearly full (correct for
byte-stream data, wrong for fixed-size message records -- a partial
write splices half a message into the ring, permanently desyncing
every message boundary after it). toygain_set_parameter_value() now
checks cuif_spsc_writable() covers a whole message before writing at
all, making it all-or-nothing. The existing Reverb plugin's
PluginEditor.cpp has this same latent hazard (unchecked
cuif_spsc_write calls) -- worth a follow-up issue, not fixed here
since it's out of this issue's scope.

Verified: full host harness (6 tests) and RT-safety stress test pass
in both Debug and Release, run multiple times to rule out flakiness
(Release: 254k process() calls, 0 regressions under real concurrent
contention).

B1: LPI Plugin ABI v1 (#106)

New PluginABI/ -- a dependency-free C header (lpi.h, extern "C",
C-linkage vtable, opaque lpi_plugin* handle), physically separate from
cuif, intended as the primary plugin format for noprod (VST3/JUCE
remains the Ableton/third-party-DAW compatibility bridge).

Core lpi_plugin_api: create/destroy/activate/deactivate/process (fixed
sample rate set once at create, matching every DSP object in this
codebase already following a one-time prepare(sampleRate, blockSize)
contract) plus parameter enumeration/get/set and versioned binary
state save/load. Growth without ABI breaks goes through a
string-ID-keyed get_extension() capability query -- a host that
doesn't know an extension id gets NULL back, a plugin that doesn't
implement one returns NULL too.

set_parameter_value() is RT-safe by ABI contract: real plugins must
internally enqueue via a lock-free SPSC ring buffer (the same pattern
Framework/include/cuif/ring_buffer.h and VSTs/Reverb/src/
PluginProcessor.cpp already use for audio<->UI communication), so
hosts never need their own locking around it.

Deliverables:
- lpi.h -- the ABI header, header-only INTERFACE CMake target
- examples/toy_gain -- a trivial single-parameter gain plugin DLL
  validating the ABI shape before Reverb's complexity is involved
- test/host_harness -- loads the toy plugin purely via LoadLibrary +
  GetProcAddress("lpi_get_factory"), no compile-time link dependency,
  proving dynamic loading end to end
- test/rt_safety -- a real two-thread stress test (one producer thread
  hammering set_parameter_value with 200k sequential values while the
  main thread calls process() in a tight loop), asserting the applied
  value sequence is always monotonic and never torn

Found and fixed a real bug during RT-safety testing: cuif_spsc_write()
does partial writes when the ring buffer is nearly full (correct for
byte-stream data, wrong for fixed-size message records -- a partial
write splices half a message into the ring, permanently desyncing
every message boundary after it). toygain_set_parameter_value() now
checks cuif_spsc_writable() covers a whole message before writing at
all, making it all-or-nothing. The existing Reverb plugin's
PluginEditor.cpp has this same latent hazard (unchecked
cuif_spsc_write calls) -- worth a follow-up issue, not fixed here
since it's out of this issue's scope.

Verified: full host harness (6 tests) and RT-safety stress test pass
in both Debug and Release, run multiple times to rule out flakiness
(Release: 254k process() calls, 0 regressions under real concurrent
contention).

Louard","email":"mbf.louard@gmail.com","date":"2026-07-17T22:52:14Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-07-17T22:52:14Z"},"message":"Drop the unused depth+stencil buffer (#105)

Every pixel-format request in window_win32.c (the shared root-context
dummy probe, the plain fallback path, and the WGL_ARB_multisample
path) asked for 24-bit depth + 8-bit stencil, but GL_DEPTH_TEST is
explicitly disabled every frame and nothing anywhere in Framework/src/
touches the stencil buffer -- cuif is a pure 2D renderer.

This was genuine unused per-window backing-store cost, multiplied 4x
under MSAA. Confirmed via #95's real per-instance memory measurement
that this framebuffer/depth/stencil cost -- not shareable via
wglShareLists, since it's intrinsically tied to each window's own
drawable -- is the dominant per-instance memory driver #91 couldn't
touch, making this a real reduction rather than a theoretical one.

Verified via full cuif_tests (25/25) and dsp_tests (9/9) suites, plus
a rebuilt Standalone screenshot confirming no visual regression
(expected -- nothing reads depth or stencil).

Drop the unused depth+stencil buffer (#105)

Every pixel-format request in window_win32.c (the shared root-context
dummy probe, the plain fallback path, and the WGL_ARB_multisample
path) asked for 24-bit depth + 8-bit stencil, but GL_DEPTH_TEST is
explicitly disabled every frame and nothing anywhere in Framework/src/
touches the stencil buffer -- cuif is a pure 2D renderer.

This was genuine unused per-window backing-store cost, multiplied 4x
under MSAA. Confirmed via #95's real per-instance memory measurement
that this framebuffer/depth/stencil cost -- not shareable via
wglShareLists, since it's intrinsically tied to each window's own
drawable -- is the dominant per-instance memory driver #91 couldn't
touch, making this a real reduction rather than a theoretical one.

Verified via full cuif_tests (25/25) and dsp_tests (9/9) suites, plus
a rebuilt Standalone screenshot confirming no visual regression
(expected -- nothing reads depth or stencil).

Part of #90.

Louard","email":"mbf.louard@gmail.com","date":"2026-07-17T22:31:48Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-07-17T22:31:48Z"},"message":"Scope juce_audio_utils to the Standalone target only (#104)

juce_audio_utils (and its transitive juce_audio_devices/juce_audio_formats
-- ASIO/WASAPI backends, FLAC/OggVorbis/AIFF/WAV codec tables) was linked
into the shared LoudioReverb target, which both the VST3 and Standalone
build outputs depend on. The VST3 format never touches an audio device
or file codec directly (the host owns I/O), and nothing in src/
references juce_audio_utils at all -- only Standalone's
AudioDeviceManager/StandaloneFilterWindow glue needs it.

juce_add_plugin() produces independently-linkable LoudioReverb_VST3 and
LoudioReverb_Standalone targets, confirmed by reading JUCEUtils.cmake
directly. Moves juce::juce_audio_utils onto LoudioReverb_Standalone
specifically.

Verified via real build: juce_audio_utils.cpp/juce_audio_devices.cpp/
juce_audio_formats.cpp/juce_gui_extra.cpp now only compile during the
Standalone target's build pass, not VST3's. Standalone relaunched and
screenshot-verified -- no audio-device error dialog, rendering
unaffected.

author Michael Louard <mbf.louard@gmail.com> 1784327508 -0400
committer GitHub <noreply@github.com> 1784327508 -0400

Scope juce_audio_utils to the Standalone target only (#104)

juce_audio_utils (and its transitive juce_audio_devices/juce_audio_formats
-- ASIO/WASAPI backends, FLAC/OggVorbis/AIFF/WAV codec tables) was linked
into the shared LoudioReverb target, which both the VST3 and Standalone
build outputs depend on. The VST3 format never touches an audio device
or file codec directly (the host owns I/O), and nothing in src/
references juce_audio_utils at all -- only Standalone's
AudioDeviceManager/StandaloneFilterWindow glue needs it.

juce_add_plugin() produces independently-linkable LoudioReverb_VST3 and
LoudioReverb_Standalone targets, confirmed by reading JUCEUtils.cmake
directly. Moves juce::juce_audio_utils onto LoudioReverb_Standalone
specifically.

Verified via real build: juce_audio_utils.cpp/juce_audio_devices.cpp/
juce_audio_formats.cpp/juce_gui_extra.cpp now only compile during the
Standalone target's build pass, not VST3's. Standalone relaunched and
screenshot-verified -- no audio-device error dialog, rendering
unaffected.

Part of #90.

Louard","email":"mbf.louard@gmail.com","date":"2026-07-17T20:58:48Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-07-17T20:58:48Z"},"message":"Fix cuif_global_font lifecycle (reopen-in-place fix) (#103)

cuif_global_font was never reset to NULL when the last window closed,
even though its GL texture was destroyed along with that window's
context. On reopen, ensureCuifWindowCreated()'s "already loaded" guard
saw a non-NULL pointer and skipped reloading the font, so it bound a
texture_id that no longer existed in the new context -- the same
dangling-texture symptom as #88, just triggered by a single instance's
close/reopen cycle instead of two simultaneous instances.

Frees the font and resets the pointer inside the same cuif_window_count
== 0 teardown block introduced in #91/#102, so it's only ever alive
while at least one window exists anywhere in the process. The root
context is made current just for the glDeleteTextures() call, since by
this point the window that brought the count to zero already had its
own context deleted.

New automated test drives the exact mechanism the plugin editor's guard
relies on: cuif_global_font must be NULL once zero windows exist.

Part of #90, #97.

author Michael Louard <mbf.louard@gmail.com> 1784321928 -0400
committer GitHub <noreply@github.com> 1784321928 -0400

Fix cuif_global_font lifecycle (reopen-in-place fix) (#103)

cuif_global_font was never reset to NULL when the last window closed,
even though its GL texture was destroyed along with that window's
context. On reopen, ensureCuifWindowCreated()'s "already loaded" guard
saw a non-NULL pointer and skipped reloading the font, so it bound a
texture_id that no longer existed in the new context -- the same
dangling-texture symptom as #88, just triggered by a single instance's
close/reopen cycle instead of two simultaneous instances.

Frees the font and resets the pointer inside the same cuif_window_count
== 0 teardown block introduced in #91/#102, so it's only ever alive
while at least one window exists anywhere in the process. The root
context is made current just for the glDeleteTextures() call, since by
this point the window that brought the count to zero already had its
own context deleted.

New automated test drives the exact mechanism the plugin editor's guard
relies on: cuif_global_font must be NULL once zero windows exist.

Part of #90, #97.

Louard","email":"mbf.louard@gmail.com","date":"2026-07-17T20:54:50Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-07-17T20:54:50Z"},"message":"Share GL object namespace via a persistent root HGLRC (closes #88) (#102)

Every cuif_window previously got a fully independent GL context with no
wglShareLists() anywhere in the codebase, so a texture baked in one
window's context (the font atlas, in particular) never existed in any
other window's context. This is what made cuif_global_font's texture
render as blank/garbage in every plugin instance after the first (#88).

Repurposes the existing MSAA-probe dummy window into a process-wide root
HGLRC, created lazily on the first cuif_window_create() and kept alive
(rather than torn down) for the life of the process. Every per-window
context now shares its GL object namespace with that root via
wglShareLists(). Teardown piggybacks on the existing cuif_window_count
refcount block, so no new lifecycle hook is needed.

New regression test exercises the real driver (glIsTexture across two
live windows), not a mock.

Part of #90, #97.

committer GitHub <noreply@github.com> 1784321690 -0400

Share GL object namespace via a persistent root HGLRC (closes #88) (#102)

Every cuif_window previously got a fully independent GL context with no
wglShareLists() anywhere in the codebase, so a texture baked in one
window's context (the font atlas, in particular) never existed in any
other window's context. This is what made cuif_global_font's texture
render as blank/garbage in every plugin instance after the first (#88).

Repurposes the existing MSAA-probe dummy window into a process-wide root
HGLRC, created lazily on the first cuif_window_create() and kept alive
(rather than torn down) for the life of the process. Every per-window
context now shares its GL object namespace with that root via
wglShareLists(). Teardown piggybacks on the existing cuif_window_count
refcount block, so no new lifecycle hook is needed.

New regression test exercises the real driver (glIsTexture across two
live windows), not a mock.

Part of #90, #97.

Louard","email":"mbf.louard@gmail.com","date":"2026-07-17T14:34:09Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-07-17T14:34:09Z"},"message":"Rebuild font atlas with oversampling, mipmaps, and DPI re-bake (#83) (#89)

Root cause #4 of the rendering-quality overhaul, and the last of the
five: cuif_font_load used the legacy stbtt_BakeFontBitmap API (no
oversampling support), baked once at a hardcoded 13px into a fixed
512x512 atlas, GL_LINEAR filtering only, no mipmaps -- text was soft
and illegible at small sizes and couldn't get crisper without a full
re-bake at a higher effective size.

- Switched to stbtt_PackBegin/PackSetOversampling/PackFontRange/PackEnd
  with 2x2 oversampling and a 1024x1024 atlas (up from 512x512, sized
  for realistic DPI scales with oversampling). Actually checks
  stbtt_PackFontRange's per-range return value now, unlike the old
  coarse `result <= 0` check.
- New cuif_font_effective_bake_px(design_px, dpi_scale): the real bake
  size (design_px * dpi_scale, clamped to a sane ceiling) -- NOTE
  oversampling does not further multiply this value, it's a packer-
  internal subpixel-quality setting, not a size multiplier (an earlier
  draft of this conflated the two).
- Mipmaps via glGenerateMipmap, GL_LINEAR_MIPMAP_LINEAR minification --
  GL 3.0 core, not in the GL 1.1-era gl/gl.h Windows ships, resolved at
  runtime via wglGetProcAddress (same pattern as #80's MSAA bootstrap),
  with a plain GL_LINEAR fallback if resolution fails.
- New cuif_font_rebake(), wired from PluginEditor.cpp's existing #79
  DPI-scale debounce logic -- text re-bakes at the new effective size
  when the display scale changes (same ~250ms debounce, no new timer).
- New cuif_font_measure_text(): widget.c's 3 knob/button/tabbar label
  centering call sites were using hardcoded per-character pixel-width
  guesses (label_len * 3.5f/7.0f) eyeballed against the old 13px bake;
  a rebuilt atlas at a different effective size would have made these
  visibly wrong. Now uses real glyph advance metrics.
- Opportunistic fix: cuif_window_create() now calls wglMakeCurrent()
  immediately after context creation, not just on the first render
  frame. cuif_font_load() runs before any window's first render call,
  so its texture upload was previously executing with no GL context
  current at all (undefined behavior that happened to be silently
  tolerated by this driver rather than a guarantee) -- found while
  investigating where to hook the new mipmap-generation extension
  resolution.
- Filed #88 (not fixed here, out of scope): the shared cuif_global_font
  texture isn't valid across multiple plugin instances' independent,
  unshared GL contexts -- a pre-existing architectural gap surfaced
  while rewriting this file, needs wglShareLists or a per-window atlas.

Verified: 23/23 cuif_tests pass (adds 3 tests for
cuif_font_effective_bake_px: identity at scale=1, linear scaling,

bar and a knob's label -- text is now genuinely legible ("Main",

author Michael Louard <mbf.louard@gmail.com> 1784298849 -0400
committer GitHub <noreply@github.com> 1784298849 -0400

Rebuild font atlas with oversampling, mipmaps, and DPI re-bake (#83) (#89)

Root cause #4 of the rendering-quality overhaul, and the last of the
five: cuif_font_load used the legacy stbtt_BakeFontBitmap API (no
oversampling support), baked once at a hardcoded 13px into a fixed
512x512 atlas, GL_LINEAR filtering only, no mipmaps -- text was soft
and illegible at small sizes and couldn't get crisper without a full
re-bake at a higher effective size.

- Switched to stbtt_PackBegin/PackSetOversampling/PackFontRange/PackEnd
  with 2x2 oversampling and a 1024x1024 atlas (up from 512x512, sized
  for realistic DPI scales with oversampling). Actually checks
  stbtt_PackFontRange's per-range return value now, unlike the old
  coarse `result <= 0` check.
- New cuif_font_effective_bake_px(design_px, dpi_scale): the real bake
  size (design_px * dpi_scale, clamped to a sane ceiling) -- NOTE
  oversampling does not further multiply this value, it's a packer-
  internal subpixel-quality setting, not a size multiplier (an earlier
  draft of this conflated the two).
- Mipmaps via glGenerateMipmap, GL_LINEAR_MIPMAP_LINEAR minification --
  GL 3.0 core, not in the GL 1.1-era gl/gl.h Windows ships, resolved at
  runtime via wglGetProcAddress (same pattern as #80's MSAA bootstrap),
  with a plain GL_LINEAR fallback if resolution fails.
- New cuif_font_rebake(), wired from PluginEditor.cpp's existing #79
  DPI-scale debounce logic -- text re-bakes at the new effective size
  when the display scale changes (same ~250ms debounce, no new timer).
- New cuif_font_measure_text(): widget.c's 3 knob/button/tabbar label
  centering call sites were using hardcoded per-character pixel-width
  guesses (label_len * 3.5f/7.0f) eyeballed against the old 13px bake;
  a rebuilt atlas at a different effective size would have made these
  visibly wrong. Now uses real glyph advance metrics.
- Opportunistic fix: cuif_window_create() now calls wglMakeCurrent()
  immediately after context creation, not just on the first render
  frame. cuif_font_load() runs before any window's first render call,
  so its texture upload was previously executing with no GL context
  current at all (undefined behavior that happened to be silently
  tolerated by this driver rather than a guarantee) -- found while
  investigating where to hook the new mipmap-generation extension
  resolution.
- Filed #88 (not fixed here, out of scope): the shared cuif_global_font
  texture isn't valid across multiple plugin instances' independent,
  unshared GL contexts -- a pre-existing architectural gap surfaced
  while rewriting this file, needs wglShareLists or a per-window atlas.

Verified: 23/23 cuif_tests pass (adds 3 tests for
cuif_font_effective_bake_px: identity at scale=1, linear scaling,
ceiling clamp for a pathological scale). Built VST3/Standalone/
dsp_tests clean. Screenshotted the Standalone build zoomed on the tab
bar and a knob's label -- text is now genuinely legible ("Main",
"Advanced", "Pre-Delay", "Damping"), a dramatic change from the
illegible blur in every earlier screenshot this epic started from.
Sent a real tab-switch click post-change; process stayed responsive.

Louard","email":"mbf.louard@gmail.com","date":"2026-07-17T14:22:15Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-07-17T14:22:15Z"},"message":"Add quad-based stroke rendering, replacing glLineWidth (#82) (#87)

Root cause #3 of the rendering-quality overhaul: every "thick" line in
the UI (knob arcs, slider tracks, the bezier decay-EQ curve, the
analyzer trace) was drawn via GL_LINES/GL_LINE_STRIP + glLineWidth().
glLineWidth() for values above 1.0 is driver-dependent and commonly
clamped in a legacy compatibility-profile GL context (the kind this
framework creates), so requested stroke thickness was never reliably
what actually rendered.

New cuif_draw_polyline(points, count, thickness, color, closed) in
graphics.h/graphics.c: per-segment perpendicular-offset quads with
bevel joins at each interior vertex, instead of glLineWidth. Routes
through all 5 places that used to draw thick lines directly:
- cuif_draw_line (now a 2-point cuif_draw_polyline call)
- cuif_draw_bezier (its curve-flattening loop now fills a point array
  first, then one cuif_draw_polyline call)
- draw_arc (widget.c) -- knob track/value arcs
- the bezier-editor's curve stroke (widget.c, was an inline
  GL_LINE_STRIP + glLineWidth(3.0f) that bypassed every shared helper)
- the analyzer's trace stroke (widget.c, same bypass pattern,
  glLineWidth(2.0f))

Also lands the arc-point-generation consolidation deferred from #81:
new cuif_generate_arc_points() (graphics.h/graphics.c) is now the
single source of adaptively-tessellated arc points, used by both
cuif_draw_rounded_rect's corners and draw_arc's stroke -- draw_arc no
longer has its own copy of the angle-stepping loop.

Verified: 20/20 cuif_tests pass (adds a geometry-correctness test for
cuif_generate_arc_points: start/end points land exactly on the
requested angles, every generated point lies on the circle). Built
VST3/Standalone/dsp_tests clean. Screenshotted the Standalone build
zoomed on a knob and the bezier curve -- clean, consistent-thickness
strokes with no gaps or artifacts. Dragged a bezier node to create a
real angled joint (not just a flat line) and re-screenshotted -- the
bevel joins hold up correctly through actual curvature, not just the
degenerate flat case. Sent real interaction (tab switch, node drag)
throughout; process stayed responsive.

author Michael Louard <mbf.louard@gmail.com> 1784298135 -0400
committer GitHub <noreply@github.com> 1784298135 -0400

Add quad-based stroke rendering, replacing glLineWidth (#82) (#87)

Root cause #3 of the rendering-quality overhaul: every "thick" line in
the UI (knob arcs, slider tracks, the bezier decay-EQ curve, the
analyzer trace) was drawn via GL_LINES/GL_LINE_STRIP + glLineWidth().
glLineWidth() for values above 1.0 is driver-dependent and commonly
clamped in a legacy compatibility-profile GL context (the kind this
framework creates), so requested stroke thickness was never reliably
what actually rendered.

New cuif_draw_polyline(points, count, thickness, color, closed) in
graphics.h/graphics.c: per-segment perpendicular-offset quads with
bevel joins at each interior vertex, instead of glLineWidth. Routes
through all 5 places that used to draw thick lines directly:
- cuif_draw_line (now a 2-point cuif_draw_polyline call)
- cuif_draw_bezier (its curve-flattening loop now fills a point array
  first, then one cuif_draw_polyline call)
- draw_arc (widget.c) -- knob track/value arcs
- the bezier-editor's curve stroke (widget.c, was an inline
  GL_LINE_STRIP + glLineWidth(3.0f) that bypassed every shared helper)
- the analyzer's trace stroke (widget.c, same bypass pattern,
  glLineWidth(2.0f))

Also lands the arc-point-generation consolidation deferred from #81:
new cuif_generate_arc_points() (graphics.h/graphics.c) is now the
single source of adaptively-tessellated arc points, used by both
cuif_draw_rounded_rect's corners and draw_arc's stroke -- draw_arc no
longer has its own copy of the angle-stepping loop.

Verified: 20/20 cuif_tests pass (adds a geometry-correctness test for
cuif_generate_arc_points: start/end points land exactly on the
requested angles, every generated point lies on the circle). Built
VST3/Standalone/dsp_tests clean. Screenshotted the Standalone build
zoomed on a knob and the bezier curve -- clean, consistent-thickness
strokes with no gaps or artifacts. Dragged a bezier node to create a
real angled joint (not just a flat line) and re-screenshotted -- the
bevel joins hold up correctly through actual curvature, not just the

Louard","email":"mbf.louard@gmail.com","date":"2026-07-17T14:13:07Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-07-17T14:13:07Z"},"message":"Add adaptive tessellation for arcs and rounded-rect corners (#81) (#86)

Root cause #2 of the rendering-quality overhaul, and the direct cause
of the "octagonal knobs" complaint: cuif_draw_rounded_rect hardcoded
8 segments/corner (graphics.c) and widget.c's draw_arc hardcoded 32
segments/circle (used for every knob's track/value arc) regardless of
actual on-screen radius.

New cuif_arc_segment_count(radius_px, sweep_radians) in
Framework/include/cuif/tessellation.h: a chord-error-bounded formula
(the standard approach in vector renderers like Skia/NanoVG) instead
of a fixed guess. radius_px must be in DEVICE pixels
(logical_radius * dpi_scale, from #79) -- this is what makes
tessellation scale up correctly on high-DPI displays instead of the
fixed counts this replaces getting proportionally *more* faceted as
physical pixel density increases.

Tuning note: an initial 0.25px chord-error tolerance actually produced
*fewer* segments than the old fixed 32 at real knob sizes (25 vs 32
at a 70x70 knob's ~22px radius) -- a regression at today's normal 1x
scale, the exact resolution the original complaint was reported at.
Tightened to 0.05px so both the 1x and 2x cases clearly exceed the old
baseline (36 and 50 segments respectively for a 70x70 knob).
Encoded as an explicit test assertion so this can't regress silently
again.

Both cuif_draw_rounded_rect and draw_arc pull the active window's
dpi_scale via the existing cuif_current_window global + the #79
cuif_window_get_dpi_scale() accessor -- no signature changes needed.

Deliberately deferred: consolidating emit_arc (graphics.c) and
draw_arc (widget.c) into one shared arc-point-generation helper, since
#82 needs arc points as an ordered polyline for quad-based stroke
rendering anyway -- doing that consolidation now would mean
re-refactoring it again immediately after for #82's actual shape
requirements.

Verified: 19/19 cuif_tests pass (adds 3 tessellation tests: monotonic
with radius, clamped at floor/ceiling, and the regression test above).
Built cuif_harness/VST3/Standalone/dsp_tests clean. Screenshotted the
Standalone build before/after (zoomed on the hero knob) -- faceting is
visibly gone. Sent a real tab-switch click post-change to confirm
interaction still works; process stayed responsive throughout.

Add adaptive tessellation for arcs and rounded-rect corners (#81) (#86)

Root cause #2 of the rendering-quality overhaul, and the direct cause
of the "octagonal knobs" complaint: cuif_draw_rounded_rect hardcoded
8 segments/corner (graphics.c) and widget.c's draw_arc hardcoded 32
segments/circle (used for every knob's track/value arc) regardless of
actual on-screen radius.

New cuif_arc_segment_count(radius_px, sweep_radians) in
Framework/include/cuif/tessellation.h: a chord-error-bounded formula
(the standard approach in vector renderers like Skia/NanoVG) instead
of a fixed guess. radius_px must be in DEVICE pixels
(logical_radius * dpi_scale, from #79) -- this is what makes
tessellation scale up correctly on high-DPI displays instead of the
fixed counts this replaces getting proportionally *more* faceted as
physical pixel density increases.

Tuning note: an initial 0.25px chord-error tolerance actually produced
*fewer* segments than the old fixed 32 at real knob sizes (25 vs 32
at a 70x70 knob's ~22px radius) -- a regression at today's normal 1x
scale, the exact resolution the original complaint was reported at.
Tightened to 0.05px so both the 1x and 2x cases clearly exceed the old
baseline (36 and 50 segments respectively for a 70x70 knob).
Encoded as an explicit test assertion so this can't regress silently
again.

Both cuif_draw_rounded_rect and draw_arc pull the active window's
dpi_scale via the existing cuif_current_window global + the #79
cuif_window_get_dpi_scale() accessor -- no signature changes needed.

Deliberately deferred: consolidating emit_arc (graphics.c) and
draw_arc (widget.c) into one shared arc-point-generation helper, since
#82 needs arc points as an ordered polyline for quad-based stroke
rendering anyway -- doing that consolidation now would mean
re-refactoring it again immediately after for #82's actual shape
requirements.

Verified: 19/19 cuif_tests pass (adds 3 tessellation tests: monotonic
with radius, clamped at floor/ceiling, and the regression test above).
Built cuif_harness/VST3/Standalone/dsp_tests clean. Screenshotted the
Standalone build before/after (zoomed on the hero knob) -- faceting is
visibly gone. Sent a real tab-switch click post-change to confirm
interaction still works; process stayed responsive throughout.

# Background
## Professionally
I work in banking, which is largely why none of my analytical toolset can be displayed here. Recently migrated Unsecured Installment loans from a legacy mainframe to one we own, so I have skills, you'll just have to trust me :). 
I'm a business analytics consultant, but that means nothing anymore. I optimize processes. I believe everyone always has, but now I do it very well, with the assist of agents. 
## Personally
I'm sort of obsessed with creating my own Digital Audio Workstation and every thing that goes into that. That work is available on my profile, and probably largely what gets contributed here for the time being. 
## Extras
Before AI I was a dreamer, now I am lazy if my dreams don't actually ship.

Louard","email":"mbl63@case.edu","date":"2026-07-16T22:55:28Z"},"committer":{"name":"Michael Louard","email":"mbl63@case.edu","date":"2026-07-16T22:55:28Z"},"message":"Make VST folder scan real: enumerate and persist actual plugins (#26)

Replaces the fake "Rescanning... Complete!" alert with a real scan:
the user picks a folder via the File System Access API and the app
recursively enumerates actual plugin binaries (.vst3/.dll/.clap/
.component), recording name, format, and path. The discovered list
persists in localStorage across sessions, de-duplicates by path, and
each plugin can be added to the selected track's device chain
(carrying its pluginPath for future native hosting). Remaining for
#26: loading scanned plugins in Audio Core (JUCE) for actual DSP —
audio_core scans VST3s at startup but doesn't yet expose hosting over
its WebSocket protocol.

Louard","email":"mbl63@case.edu","date":"2026-07-16T22:52:25Z"},"committer":{"name":"Michael Louard","email":"mbl63@case.edu","date":"2026-07-16T22:52:25Z"},"message":"Add punch in/out recording controls (closes #36)

A punch strip sits atop the arrangement timeline: drag to set the
punch region (snapped to beats), with a PUNCH toggle in the transport.
While playback runs with punch enabled, the playhead loop engages the
record state when crossing the punch-in point and disengages it at
punch-out, so recording no longer depends on manually timing the
record button. (Recording itself remains the app's arm/record state —
actual audio input capture is a separate future feature.)

Louard","email":"mbl63@case.edu","date":"2026-07-16T22:47:06Z"},"committer":{"name":"Michael Louard","email":"mbl63@case.edu","date":"2026-07-16T22:47:06Z"},"message":"Add audio warping: transient detection + granular time-stretch (closes #17)

Enabling Warp in Clip View runs energy-based transient detection on
the clip and estimates its native tempo from the median transient
interval (folded into 70-180 BPM). Warped clips render at their
tempo-stretched length with transient markers overlaid, and play back
through a granular overlap-add scheduler (90ms grains, 50% crossfade)
so they follow the project tempo without repitching. Changing the
project BPM re-stretches warped clips both visually and audibly.
Warp mode (Beats/Tones/Texture) and original BPM are editable per clip.

Louard","email":"mbl63@case.edu","date":"2026-07-16T22:42:11Z"},"committer":{"name":"Michael Louard","email":"mbl63@case.edu","date":"2026-07-16T22:42:11Z"},"message":"Add track freeze & flatten via offline rendering (closes #29)

Freeze renders a track's clips through an OfflineAudioContext (MIDI
notes are rendered through the track's synth instrument; audio clips
with their trim/offset) and stores the buffer on the track. While
frozen: playback uses the rendered buffer instead of live clips, and
the device chain panel is locked with a Frozen banner. Flatten
permanently replaces the track's clips, instrument, and devices with
the frozen audio and converts the track to an audio track. Both are
undoable. Note: chain effects are still UI-only (no DSP until
#18-#21), so renders cover instrument + clip audio.

Louard","email":"mbl63@case.edu","date":"2026-07-16T22:39:06Z"},"committer":{"name":"Michael Louard","email":"mbl63@case.edu","date":"2026-07-16T22:39:06Z"},"message":"Add Audio Effect Rack with macro mappings and presets (closes #22)

Racks are container devices in a track's chain holding nested devices
plus four macro knobs. Each macro carries mappings (device, parameter,
min..max); sweeping the macro drives every mapped parameter across its
range. Devices can be grouped into a rack ("Group into Rack" wraps the
track's loose devices), added/removed inside the rack, and racks can
be saved as presets and instantiated on any track (deep-cloned with
fresh ids and remapped macro targets).

Louard","email":"mbl63@case.edu","date":"2026-07-16T22:35:26Z"},"committer":{"name":"Michael Louard","email":"mbl63@case.edu","date":"2026-07-16T22:35:26Z"},"message":"Make group tracks real folder tracks with summing (closes #28)

Group tracks now fold/unfold via a chevron (members hide from the
arranger, mixer headers, and session view while their audio keeps
playing), show a member count, and get a purple-tinted header. The
audio graph now honors grouping: member track gains are patched into
the group track's gain node (true summing bus, so group volume/mute
affects all members) instead of every track connecting straight to
master, with re-patching only when a track's routing target changes.
Also exposes the store on window.__dawStore in dev builds for
console/automation access.

Louard","email":"mbl63@case.edu","date":"2026-07-16T22:29:46Z"},"committer":{"name":"Michael Louard","email":"mbl63@case.edu","date":"2026-07-16T22:29:46Z"},"message":"Add master bus metering and brickwall limiter (closes #34)

Master chain is now tracks -> master gain -> limiter -> analyser ->
output. The limiter is a DynamicsCompressor configured brickwall
(-1dB threshold, 20:1, 1ms attack) with a LIM bypass toggle; the
analyser stays in-chain either way so the meter always shows what
reaches the speakers. Control bar gains a live peak meter
(green/yellow/red zones), dB readout, latching click-to-reset clip
LED, and a gain-reduction readout while the limiter works.

Louard","email":"mbl63@case.edu","date":"2026-07-16T22:27:23Z"},"committer":{"name":"Michael Louard","email":"mbl63@case.edu","date":"2026-07-16T22:27:23Z"},"message":"Add Piano Roll MIDI note editor in Clip View (closes #16)

Double-clicking a MIDI clip opens a piano-roll editor in the bottom
panel (which grows to fit): a C2-C6 note grid with 1/16 snapping,
double-click to draw notes, drag to move/transpose (with audition on
pitch change), right-edge drag to resize, double-click or Delete key
to remove, and a velocity lane with draggable bars. Edits persist
through updateRegionNotes and participate in undo/redo. Also fixes the
Device Chain / Clip View tabs, which previously had no click handlers.
